skill.name: bark-notify
skill.description: Send Bark push notifications when Codex, Claude, or Claude Code tasks finish, with project context, status, and a short summary.
skill.summary: Send Bark mobile alerts when AI agent tasks complete.
skill.author: caopulan

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm malicious intent or prompt injection. The critical static heuristic is explained by the skill purpose: it runs a local helper, reads notification configuration, and sends a Bark push request. Publish with a warning because task summaries and the Bark key can leave the local environment.

medium_findings.title: Task Summary Sent to External Push Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The helper builds a notification body from machine name, project name, status, and summary, then posts it to the Bark endpoint. This is the intended feature, but summaries can leak sensitive task details if the caller includes secrets. Confidence: 0.86.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network request and payload construction are explicit, but the behavior is legitimate for a notification skill when users avoid sensitive content.

medium_findings.title: Dry Run Can Print Bark Key
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The Bark key is embedded in the URL path, and dry-run mode prints the full POST URL. Terminal logs or copied output could expose the notification token. Confidence: 0.93.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs the URL with CODEX_BARK_KEY and prints that URL during dry-run execution.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variables Control Notification Credentials and Endpoint
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate context. The script reads CODEX_MACHINE_NAME, CODEX_BARK_KEY, and CODEX_BARK_BASE_URL from the environment. This is normal configuration, but a changed base URL can redirect notification contents. Confidence: 0.78.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is explicit and necessary, while the base URL override creates a bounded but real data-routing risk.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Command Invokes Local Helper Script
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for command injection. The Markdown shows a fixed python3 command for the installed helper script with placeholder arguments. I did not find evidence of untrusted input being interpolated into a shell command by the skill code. Confidence: 0.95.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged command text is documentation, and the Python script uses argparse rather than shell evaluation.

low_findings.title: Local Project Metadata Read From AGENTS.md
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with low severity. The helper searches the current directory and parent directories for AGENTS.md and reads it to extract a project name. This is limited filesystem access and does not exfiltrate file contents except the derived project name in the notification body. Confidence: 0.82.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is explicit and narrowly scoped to AGENTS.md, with only a parsed name included in the notification.

low_findings.title: Static Heuristic Findings Mostly Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detections point to descriptive text, not cryptographic code. The path traversal and hidden-file detections are Markdown examples for ~/.codex and placeholder ellipses, not runtime traversal logic. Confidence: 0.98.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are human-readable descriptions or usage examples, and no weak cryptographic primitive or path traversal operation appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Task Summary Sent to External Push Service
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The helper builds a notification body from machine name, project name, status, and summary, then posts it to the Bark endpoint. This is the intended feature, but summaries can leak sensitive task details if the caller includes secrets. Confidence: 0.86.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request and payload construction are explicit, but the behavior is legitimate for a notification skill when users avoid sensitive content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dry Run Can Print Bark Key
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The Bark key is embedded in the URL path, and dry-run mode prints the full POST URL. Terminal logs or copied output could expose the notification token. Confidence: 0.93.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs the URL with CODEX_BARK_KEY and prints that URL during dry-run execution.

content.value_statement: Long AI agent runs can finish while the user is away from the terminal. This skill sends Bark mobile alerts with task status, project context, and a short completion summary.
actual_capabilities: Sends a Bark push notification after an AI agent task finishes.
Includes device name, project name, status, and summary in the notification body.
Reads Bark key, device name, and base URL from environment variables.
Detects a project name from AGENTS.md frontmatter or labeled project lines.
Supports dry-run mode and configurable request timeout for testing.
limitations: Requires a valid Bark key and network access to the configured Bark service.
Notification summaries are sent to an external service and should not contain secrets.
Does not include retry logic, delivery confirmation, or offline queueing.
Project name detection is limited to supported AGENTS.md patterns.

use_cases.title: Monitor Long Coding Runs
use_cases.description: Receive a mobile alert when a Codex implementation or test run completes.

use_cases.title: Track Remote Agent Work
use_cases.description: Send task status from a remote workstation to a phone through Bark.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Review Handoffs
use_cases.description: Notify a teammate when an AI-assisted analysis is done and ready for review.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Completion Alert
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single task notification.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bark-notify when this task is complete. Send status success if all requested work is finished, and include a short summary.

prompt_templates.title: Failure-Aware Alert
prompt_templates.scenario: Notify with clear status when work is blocked or partially complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: After the task ends, use bark-notify with status success, partial, failed, or blocked. Keep the summary brief and do not include secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Project-Specific Alert
prompt_templates.scenario: Override or confirm project naming for clearer mobile notifications.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bark-notify after this run. Set the project name to the current repository name unless AGENTS.md defines a project name.

prompt_templates.title: Operational Run Notification
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for long runs with test and deployment context.
prompt_templates.prompt: When the full workflow completes, send a Bark notification with the final status, project name, and a concise summary of implementation, tests, and remaining blockers.

output_examples.input: Notify me when the refactor is complete.
output_examples.output: A mobile Bark alert reports the project name, success status, and a short refactor summary.

output_examples.input: Send an alert if the test run fails.
output_examples.output: A Bark notification marks the run as failed and summarizes the failing step without exposing sensitive output.

output_examples.input: Use dry run to preview the notification.
output_examples.output: The terminal shows the notification request details so the user can verify title, status, and summary before sending.

best_practices: Keep notification summaries short and free of tokens, passwords, customer data, or private logs.
Set CODEX_BARK_BASE_URL only to a trusted Bark endpoint before enabling automatic alerts.
Use dry-run mode during setup, then avoid sharing terminal output that contains the Bark URL.
anti_patterns: Do not include raw error logs, access tokens, or private repository details in summaries.
Do not point the base URL to an untrusted server or shared test endpoint.
Do not treat Bark delivery as proof that a task succeeded; verify critical results separately.

faq.question: What does this skill send?
faq.answer: It sends a Bark push notification with device name, project name, status, and a short task summary.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it need a Bark account or key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires CODEX_BARK_KEY and CODEX_MACHINE_NAME in the environment.

faq.question: Can it use a self-hosted Bark server?
faq.answer: Yes. CODEX_BARK_BASE_URL can point to another trusted Bark-compatible endpoint.

faq.question: Does it read project files?
faq.answer: It searches for AGENTS.md and reads supported project name fields when no project name is provided.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive work?
faq.answer: Use caution. Notification summaries leave the local machine, so sensitive details should be omitted.
